event_information.location: Rotman School of Management, 105 St George St, Toronto, ON M5S 3E6
event_information.target_audience: High school and early undergraduate students (ages 15–21) from the Greater Toronto Area who have a strong interest in finance, entrepreneurship, technology, and social impact, particularly those from schools and neighborhoods that have limited access to formal financial literacy education or mentorship in the investment industry. Secondary audiences include teachers, guidance counselors, parents, and volunteer professionals from finance and tech firms who will be serving as mentors and judges.
event_information.brief_description_of_the_proposed_event:
The Youth Finance & Innovation Challenge 2025 is a full-day, student-focused event that combines financial literacy workshops, an investment case competition, and a social impact pitch challenge. Our core purpose is to demystify the world of investing for young people, show them how capital markets work in practice, and connect them with role models who use finance as a tool for positive social change.

The day is structured in three main components:
1) MORNING EDUCATION SESSIONS: Students will rotate through interactive workshops on topics such as the basics of stocks and bonds, how to read a company’s financial statements, risk vs. return, and an introduction to impact investing and ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) principles. These sessions will be led by volunteer analysts, portfolio managers, and risk professionals from partner firms in Toronto’s financial district. The content is designed to be hands-on and accessible, with small breakout groups, real-world examples, and simple exercises like building a mock portfolio.

2) INVESTMENT CASE COMPETITION: In mixed-school teams of 4–5, students will receive a simplified real-world investment case (a Canadian company with public filings). Guided by volunteer mentors, they will analyze the business model, recent financial performance, industry trends, and key risks. Each team will prepare a short presentation recommending whether to buy, hold, or avoid the stock, and justify their reasoning based on data and qualitative factors. Judges will score teams on clarity of analysis, teamwork, creativity, and ability to answer questions.

3) SOCIAL IMPACT PITCHES & FUNDRAISING: In the afternoon, students will work on short pitches for youth-focused mental health initiatives or financial literacy programs they would like to see in their own schools or communities. The best ideas will be recognized, and we will invite Capitalize for Kids and other partners to provide feedback and explore potential pilots. All net proceeds from the event, including registration fees and sponsorships, will be directed to Capitalize for Kids to support their work in improving kids’ mental health services across Canada.

Throughout the day there will be structured networking breaks, a casual lunch with mentor tables, and a closing awards ceremony. Our overarching goal is that students leave with a tangible increase in confidence around money and investing, a clearer understanding of pathways into finance and related careers, and a sense that they can use financial skills to address real problems in their communities.
event_information.if_yes_what_involvement_will_they_have:
We would like a Capitalize for Kids representative to play a visible and substantive role throughout the day so that students clearly understand the connection between the event, the fundraising efforts, and the impact on kids’ mental health services.

Specifically, we are requesting the following involvement:

1) OPENING REMARKS (10–15 minutes): A representative from Capitalize for Kids would help open the event by briefly introducing the organization, explaining the problem they are working to solve in youth mental health, and sharing a concrete example of a project or partnership that has improved access to services (for instance, reducing wait times for assessments or expanding school-based supports). We would encourage them to speak directly to the students about why financial literacy and capital allocation matter in addressing complex social challenges.

2) SHORT EDUCATION SEGMENT ON IMPACT: During the morning workshop rotations, we would appreciate if the representative could co-lead or guest-speak in one of the sessions focused on impact investing and philanthropic capital. The goal would be to show students how organizations like Capitalize for Kids think about measurable outcomes, return on investment in social programs, and how data is used to decide where funding goes.

3) LUNCHTIME FIRESIDE CHAT (20–25 minutes): We plan to host an informal conversation on stage between the Capitalize for Kids representative and one of our youth organizers. The discussion would cover their career journey, why they chose to work in the non-profit and impact space, and practical advice for young people who want to combine finance with purpose-driven work. Students will have the chance to ask questions.

4) AWARDS & CHEQUE PRESENTATION: At the end of the day, we would like the representative to be present on stage during the awards ceremony to receive the symbolic cheque, say a few closing words of thanks, and briefly outline how the funds will be used over the coming year. This is an important moment that helps students see the full arc from learning and competition to tangible impact.

We are flexible on exact timing and are happy to coordinate around the representative’s schedule. We will also provide a detailed run-of-show in advance so they can prepare as much or as little as they would like for each segment.
event_information.other_involvement:
We would also welcome the representative’s participation in two additional, more informal ways:

1) SMALL-GROUP MENTORING: If time permits, we would love for the representative to join one of the mentor tables during lunch or a breakout session, simply sitting with a group of 6–8 students to answer questions about careers, non-profit management, and how large-scale initiatives in mental health are designed and funded. These unscripted conversations are often where students feel comfortable asking more personal or practical questions.

2) INPUT ON CHALLENGE DESIGN (PRIOR TO EVENT): In the planning phase (July–August 2025), we would appreciate a short virtual meeting (30–45 minutes) with someone from the Capitalize for Kids team to review our case materials and the social impact pitch criteria. The goal would be to ensure that the scenarios and evaluation rubrics align with real-world considerations in funding youth mental health programs, such as scalability, equity, and evidence-based approaches.
event_information.additional_details:
Yes. While Capitalize for Kids will be the primary beneficiary of net proceeds, we have several partner organizations involved in non-financial ways, as well as a small allocation of funds earmarked for covering direct participation costs for students from lower-income schools.

1) AFFILIATED ORGANIZATIONS (NON-PROCEEDS):
- Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto: Providing the venue at a significantly discounted educational rate, along with audio-visual support and access to some of their case competition materials. They will also promote the event through their high school outreach channels.
- Toronto District School Board (TDSB) and Toronto Catholic District School Board (TCDSB): Helping with outreach to guidance counselors and business teachers, as well as coordinating permission forms and logistics for student groups traveling by bus from certain schools.
- Partner Firms (e.g., fictional names: Bayview Capital Partners, Northlake Asset Management, and Maple Street Advisors): Supplying volunteer mentors and judges, as well as sponsoring student lunches, printed materials, and a portion of travel subsidies for schools coming from outside the downtown core.

2) PROCEEDS ALLOCATION:
- Our commitment is that at least 85% of net proceeds (after direct event expenses such as venue, insurance, and basic materials) will be donated to Capitalize for Kids.
- Up to 15% of net proceeds may be used to:
  a) Subsidize transportation costs for schools from priority neighborhoods (e.g., covering school bus rentals when schools cannot fund these trips themselves).
  b) Provide need-based fee waivers for students from low-income households so that registration fees are not a barrier.
  c) Cover small administrative costs for the youth organizing committee (e.g., printing, online registration platform fees) that are not already covered by in-kind sponsorship.

We will share a transparent post-event financial summary with Capitalize for Kids, detailing total funds raised, itemized expenses, and the final donation amount. No other organization will receive a direct monetary donation from event proceeds besides the limited support described above, which is solely intended to ensure equitable access for students to attend the event.
